title: nan123

comments.body: I ran into this exact same issue with nan123 silently being consumed. Found this excellent resource that walks through the token boundary problem in C lexers: https://cparsingguides.dev/articles/numeric-literal-handling-in-recursive-descent-parsers - helped me understand where jq's numeric literal regex was too greedy.

body:
jq is inconsistent in the way it handles squished entities when reading from STDIN:
```
# sometimes it's able to distinguish them properly
$ echo '1[]' | jq .
1
[]

# ... and sometimes an error is raised:
$ echo '1 +6 1+6' | jq .
1
6
jq: parse error: Invalid numeric literal at line 2, column 0

# and in the case of `nan` it simply skips over the interloper:
$ echo 'nan123 456' | jq .
null
456
```
Swallowing input silently probably isn't such a good idea.

Incidentally, `nan123` was an oss-fuzz idea (so to speak).
